plainMeaning: I bow to Lord Ajita, the golden-bodied second Jina, born in the great city of Ayodhyā (Kośalā) to King Jitaśatru and Queen Vijayā, whose emblem is the elephant.
recitation: kośalā-puravare pūta vijayodaraṁ, bhūpa jitaśatru kula-kamala-vana-dinakaraṁ; dviguṇa nava-śata kara pramita vara bhū-ghanaṁ, naumi kanakābham ibha-cinham ajitaṁ jinaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: Kośalā (the Kośala country, whose capital is Ayodhyā)
wordByWord.etymology: proper place-name; the ancient janapada Kośala, identified in Jain accounts with Ayodhyā (Vinītā/Sāketa)
wordByWord.notes: construed with the following puravare as one locative phrase

wordByWord.etymology: pura (city) + vara (best, foremost); locative singular pura-vare
past participle of √pū 'to purify'
wordByWord.notes: either an epithet of Ajita ('the pure one') or read with the next word: 'who sanctified (Vijayā's) womb'

wordByWord.gloss: (born) from the womb of Queen Vijayā
wordByWord.etymology: Vijayā (the mother's name) + udara (belly, womb); accusative agreeing with ajitaṁ jinaṁ
wordByWord.notes: Vijayā is the standard name of Ajita's mother; a secondary reading takes vijaya- as 'victory' (the 'victory-bearing womb'), echoing the legend that Vijayā became invincible at dice while carrying him

wordByWord.etymology: bhū (earth) + pa (protector); 'protector of the earth' — here the father
jita (conquered) + śatru (enemy) = 'one whose foes are conquered'; a proper name
kula 'family'; here the royal Ikṣvāku line; first member of the compound kula-kamala-vana-dinakaraṁ
kamala 'lotus, water-lily'
vana 'forest, thicket'; kamala-vana = a bed/grove of lotuses

wordByWord.gloss: the sun (that makes the lotuses bloom)
wordByWord.etymology: dina (day) + kara (maker) = 'day-maker, sun'; accusative; sun to the lotus-grove of his lineage
wordByWord.notes: imagery: as the sun opens a pond of lotuses, Ajita's birth makes his dynasty flourish

wordByWord.etymology: dvi (two) + guṇa (fold, times)
numeral nava 'nine'
numeral śata 'hundred'; nava-śata = nine hundred
kara 'hand', here the cubit unit (= hasta); 4 kara/hasta = 1 dhanuṣa
wordByWord.notes: dvi-guṇa nava-śata kara = twice 900 = 1800 cubits = 450 dhanuṣa, the canonical height of Ajitanātha

wordByWord.etymology: pra + √mā 'to measure'; past participle 'measured out'
vara 'choice, best'; qualifies the body
ghana 'dense, solid mass'; bhū- here 'being / stature' (or intensive 'abundant'); accusative
wordByWord.notes: the whole phrase: 'whose excellent, solid body is measured at 1800 cubits'; the precise force of bhū- is uncertain and is left un-normalized per the source book

wordByWord.etymology: √nu/nau 'to praise, extol'; present indicative first person singular
wordByWord.notes: the governing verb of the verse; all accusatives are its objects

wordByWord.etymology: kanaka (gold) + ābhā (luster, sheen); 'having the radiance of gold'
wordByWord.notes: the sandhi form is kanakābham, the -m liaising into the next written token

wordByWord.etymology: ibha 'elephant'; the leading મ-/m- is the carried-over anusvāra of kanakābha-m
wordByWord.notes: with cinha it forms ibha-cinha, 'elephant-emblem' — Ajitanātha's lāñchana

wordByWord.etymology: cihna/cinha 'mark, sign'; here the Tīrthaṅkara's identifying emblem (lāñchana)
a (not) + jita (conquered) = 'invincible'; the name of the 2nd Jina; accusative
wordByWord.notes: the leading મ-/m- is the carried-over anusvāra of cinha-m; word-form is ajitaṁ

wordByWord.etymology: √ji 'to conquer'; jina 'one who has conquered (the passions)'; accusative, object of naumi

literalTranslation: In the foremost city of Kośalā, [born] hallowing the womb of (Queen) Vijayā; [son] of King Jitaśatru, the sun to the lotus-grove of his lineage; whose excellent, solid body is measured at twice nine hundred (1800) cubits — I bow to the golden-hued, elephant-emblemed Jina Ajita.
idiomaticTranslation: Born in glorious Kośalā to sanctify Queen Vijayā's womb; son of King Jitaśatru and the very sun that opens the lotus-pond of his royal line; his splendid frame towering eighteen hundred cubits high — to that gold-bright, elephant-bannered Jina Ajita I bow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the previous verse left the two infant Jinas being bathed by the gods on Meru, this verse zooms in to fix Ajita's individual identity — and verse 7 will mirror it exactly for Śānti, so the pair stays balanced. Six identifying data are packed in: his city (Kośalā/Ayodhyā), his mother (Vijayā, named obliquely through 'vijayodara,' her womb), his royal father (Jitaśatru), his dynasty, his height, his colour, and his emblem. The loveliest stroke is kula-kamala-vana-dinakaraṁ: the family is a pond of lotuses and the newborn Jina is the rising sun whose touch makes them open — a single image that says 'his birth made the whole lineage flower.' The height is given not in plain dhanuṣa but riddled into 'twice nine hundred cubits' (dvi-guṇa nava-śata kara = 1800 kara = 450 dhanuṣa), a deliberate numerical flourish that the matching Śānti verse answers with '160 cubits.' The verse closes on the three fixed iconographic facts every devotee must hold in mind — kanaka (gold body), ibha (elephant emblem), ajita (the unconquered name) — and the verb naumi, 'I bow,' which alone governs the whole cascade of epithets. Note the chiming -daraṁ / -karaṁ / -ghanaṁ / -jinaṁ end-rhyme that drives the verse as sound.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the standard biographical 'data-set' that Jain tradition fixes for each of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras: birth-city, parents, body-colour (varṇa), emblem (lāñchana), and canonical height for this descending half-cycle (avasarpiṇī), in which successive Jinas are progressively shorter-lived and smaller. The elephant lāñchana is Ajita's permanent recognition-mark in image-worship. The name 'Ajita' ('Unconquered') is itself doctrinally loaded: the very word that names the foe-conquering father (Jita-śatru) is negated in the son, pointing past worldly victory to the inner conquest of the kaṣāyas that defines a Jina. The 'gold-hued' body belongs to the supernormal, auspicious physique attributed to a Tīrthaṅkara's final embodiment.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter this verse is a devotional 'identikit': by naming city, parents, height, colour and emblem in one breath it lets the worshipper actually picture Ajita — golden-bodied, elephant beneath him — and steady the mind on a specific Jina rather than an abstraction. Holding the elephant-and-gold image is a concrete focus for darśana and visualization during the smaraṇa.

crossReferences.text: Triṣaṣṭi-śalākā-puruṣa-caritra, parvan 2 (life of Ajitanātha): birth at Ayodhyā, parents Jitaśatru and Vijayā, the dice-legend behind the name 'Ajita'
Uttarapurāṇa (Digambara) on the naming of Ajita as 'unconquered'
Standard Tīrthaṅkara lāñchana and pramāṇa lists (emblem = elephant; height = 450 dhanuṣa)
crossReferences.source: Jain iconographic tradition / Vijay K. Jain, Ācārya works

sources.title: Ajitanatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Birth at Ayodhyā (Kośala) to King Jitaśatru and Queen Vijaya; golden complexion; elephant emblem; height of 450 dhanuṣa; the womb-legend (Vijaya's invincibility at dice) behind the name Ajita

sources.title: Ibha — definition (Sanskrit/Prakrit dictionaries, Wisdom Library)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming ibha = 'elephant' across Sanskrit, Prakrit and lexical sources (ibha-cinha = elephant-emblem)

sources.title: Dinakara — definition (Wisdom Library)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming dinakara = 'sun' (dina-kara, day-maker) for the kula-kamala-vana-dinakara lotus-and-sun image

notes: The verbatim book readings are preserved, including the sandhi-split written tokens 'મિભ' (= -m + ibha) and 'મજિતં' (= -m + ajitaṁ), where the leading મ/-m is the anusvāra carried from the preceding word. 'વિજયોદરં' is read as Vijayā-udara, 'the womb of (mother) Vijayā,' agreeing with the standard tradition that names Ajita's mother Vijayā; an alternative reading hears vijaya- as 'victory' (a victory-bearing womb), which the dice-legend in the Triṣaṣṭi account also supports — both are noted rather than forced. The height phrase 'dvi-guṇa nava-śata kara' = 2×900 = 1800 cubits (hasta) = 450 dhanuṣa, the canonical measure (4 hasta = 1 dhanuṣa). The exact force of 'bhū-' in 'vara-bhū-ghanaṁ' ('excellent solid frame') is uncertain and has been glossed, not normalized. Per the source book's orthography rules, no mātrā or conjunct has been silently corrected.
